original_hypothesis.title: Fixed-Rate Pricing Model Vulnerable to Stablecoin Depeg Arbitrage
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The AMM relies on a hardcoded 1:1 pricing model (M=9970, N=9985) without any price oracle, bonding curve, or slippage adjustment. If a supported stablecoin depegs, arbitrageurs will drain the pool of the healthy token, leaving LPs with only the depegged asset.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Stablecoins frequently experience temporary or permanent depegs (e.g., USDC in 2023, UST). A fixed-rate AMM acts as a naive limit order, absorbing 100% of the impermanent loss when market prices diverge from the hardcoded rate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE; (fixed rate regardless of market price)

preconditions: One of the pooled stablecoins depegs from $1.00 by more than the 0.3% fee margin
Sufficient liquidity exists in the pool

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Price is always exactly 0.997 or 0.9985, regardless of external market value
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Arbitrageurs buy the depegged token cheaply on external markets and swap it for the healthy token at the fixed 1:1 rate in the AMM.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = (Value of healthy token extracted) - (Cost of depegged token inputted) - (0.3% fee)

economic_questions: What is the historical volatility of the supported stablecoins?
Is there any off-chain mechanism to pause the contract during a depeg?
validation_requirements: Model LP losses during a simulated 5% depeg event
Calculate the exact arbitrage threshold based on the M and N constants

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Depends on pool size; flash loans can be used
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to the depeg percentage and pool size
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Continuous as long as depeg persists

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: External market liquidity for the depegged token
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: LPs suffer severe impermanent loss, effectively holding the bag for the depegged asset

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss proportional to the depeg severity
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total value of the healthy token in the pool

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: M and N fee margins vs historical stablecoin volatility

validation_result.mechanism: fixed-rate pricing without oracle or protective mechanism
validation_result.impact_summary: Fixed-rate AMM acts as a standing limit order at ~1:1. During a stablecoin depeg, arbitrageurs drain all healthy tokens from the pool, leaving LPs holding only the depegged asset — resulting in near-total loss of LP value.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The FeeAMM implements a constant-rate swap model where executeFeeSwap always prices output at amountOut = amountIn * 9970 / 10000 (0.3% fee) and rebalanceSwap at amountIn = amountOut * 9985 / 10000 + 1 (0.15% fee). These rates are hardcoded constants with no oracle, no bonding curve, no dynamic adjustment, and no pause mechanism. When an external stablecoin depeg occurs (e.g., userToken drops to $0.95), an arbitrageur can buy the depegged token at $0.95 on external markets and swap it for the healthy validatorToken at the fixed rate of ~$0.997 within the AMM, profiting ~$0.047 per token minus gas. The pool has no mechanism to detect, adjust for, or resist this drain. The arbitrageur repeats until all reserveValidatorToken is exhausted. LPs are left holding only the depegged asset. This is a missing safeguard — the code provides zero protection against a well-documented, historically demonstrated risk (USDC depeg Mar 2023, UST May 2022). The absence of any protective mechanism (oracle check, pause function, admin-adjustable rates, circuit breaker, or bonding curve) constitutes a code defect in the form of a missing check.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal correctly identifies the fixed-rate calculation. Line ~85 of FeeAMM.sol contains `amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE;` where M=9970 and SCALE=10000, confirming a hardcoded 0.3% fee with no market-price adjustment. Similarly, rebalanceSwap uses `amountIn = (amountOut * N) / SCALE + 1` with N=9985. Neither function references any external price data, pool utilization ratio, or time-weighted average. The contract has no oracle dependency (confirmed by Phase A: oracle_dependencies is empty), no admin role, no pause function, and no governance mechanism that could adjust rates. The contract's own NatSpec describes it as handling 'TIP-20 USD stablecoins', confirming the 1:1 peg assumption is baked into the design.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: amountOut is computed using a hardcoded multiplier M=9970 regardless of market conditions. If userToken depegs to $0.95, the swap still values it at ~$0.997, creating a $0.047/token arbitrage opportunity. No oracle, bonding curve, or dynamic pricing prevents draining the healthy validatorToken reserve.

Similarly uses fixed-rate N=9985. If validatorToken depegs, arbitrageurs buy cheap validatorToken externally and use rebalanceSwap to extract healthy userToken at a fixed favorable rate. Same depeg vulnerability applies in reverse direction.

calling_sequence: 1. Arbitrageur detects stablecoin depeg (e.g., userToken drops to $0.95 on external markets)
2. Arbitrageur acquires depegged userToken on external DEX at $0.95 per token
4. amountOut = largeAmount * 9970 / 10000 — fixed rate of ~0.997 validatorToken per userToken
5. Reserve check passes: pool.reserveValidatorToken >= amountOut (pool has sufficient healthy tokens)
6. Depegged userToken transferred into pool, healthy validatorToken transferred out
7. Arbitrageur sells healthy validatorToken on external market at $1.00
10. LPs now hold pool with ~100% depegged userToken and ~0% healthy validatorToken
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point is executeFeeSwap at FeeAMM.sol line ~79. The critical line is `amountOut = (amountIn * M) / SCALE;` at ~line 85, which computes output using the hardcoded constant M=9970 with no reference to external prices. The only check before the swap executes is `pool.reserveValidatorToken < amountOut` (reserve sufficiency), which does not account for the market value of the input token. The swap proceeds at the fixed rate until reserves are exhausted. There is no circuit breaker, no maximum swap size relative to reserves, no oracle price check, and no pause mechanism. The identical pattern exists in rebalanceSwap at line ~119 with constant N=9985.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided. The foundry.toml confirms Solidity 0.8.28 compilation but provides no deployment configuration or token whitelists.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and requires only standard DeFi operations: (1) monitor stablecoin prices for depeg events, (2) acquire the depegged token cheaply on external markets (can use flash loans), (3) swap depegged tokens for healthy tokens via executeFeeSwap at the fixed ~1:1 rate, (4) sell healthy tokens at market price. The profit margin is approximately (depeg_percentage - 0.3%) per token swapped. Historical examples: USDC depegged to $0.87 in March 2023 (13% depeg = 12.7% profit margin per token). UST collapsed entirely in May 2022. The attack requires no special permissions (all functions are permissionless), no callback mechanisms, and works with standard ERC-20 tokens. Capital efficiency is maximized with flash loans. The attack is continuous as long as the depeg persists and reserves remain.
validation_result.impact_assessment: SEVERE. All LP funds in the affected pool are at risk. When a depeg occurs, the healthy token reserve is drained completely by arbitrageurs. LPs can only withdraw the depegged asset (worth less than deposited). With no pause mechanism or admin controls, there is no way to stop the drain once it begins. Total value at risk equals the entire reserveValidatorToken balance across all affected pools. The fixed-rate design means there is zero price impact for large trades, so a single arbitrageur can drain the entire reserve in one transaction (limited only by gas and uint128 bounds).
recommended_mitigations: Integrate a price oracle (e.g., Chainlink) to validate that token pairs maintain approximate peg before allowing swaps
Implement a bonding curve or CPMM (x*y=k) to create price impact and natural resistance to pool draining
Add a pause mechanism controlled by a trusted admin/multisig to halt swaps during depeg events
Implement per-block or per-epoch swap limits to slow pool drainage
Add circuit breakers that automatically pause swaps when reserve ratios exceed configurable thresholds (e.g., >80% in one asset)
Add slippage protection parameters (minAmountOut) to swap functions
Implement a maximum trade size as a percentage of pool reserves
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy FeeAMM with two mock ERC-20 stablecoins (tokenA, tokenB). 2. Mint LP via mint(tokenA, tokenB, 1_000_000e18, lpAddress) to create a pool with 1M validatorToken. 3. Execute several executeFeeSwap calls to build up userToken reserves. 4. Simulate depeg: set external price of tokenA to $0.95. 5. Call executeFeeSwap(tokenA, tokenB, maxAmount) to swap depegged tokenA for healthy tokenB at the fixed 0.997 rate. 6. Verify that the arbitrageur profits (0.997 - 0.95 = 0.047 per token). 7. Repeat until reserveValidatorToken is exhausted. 8. Verify LP burn returns primarily depegged tokenA with minimal tokenB.
uncertainties: The contract is designed specifically for stablecoin pairs where depegs are rare — the 0.3% fee margin may be considered adequate for normal stablecoin volatility (typically <0.1%)
There may be off-chain monitoring and operational procedures (e.g., LP withdrawal bots) that mitigate the risk in practice, though no on-chain protection exists
The severity depends on pool sizes and the magnitude/duration of depeg events, which are external market conditions
additional_testing_needed: Quantitative modeling of LP losses across historical depeg events (USDC Mar 2023, DAI Dec 2022)
Analysis of the exact depeg threshold at which arbitrage becomes profitable: depeg > 0.3% (M fee) for executeFeeSwap, depeg > 0.15% (N fee) for rebalanceSwap
Simulation of pool drainage rate under various depeg magnitudes
Assessment of whether flash loan amplification changes the attack economics
